---
title: "Journal Entry 2 — Coding Agent MRT progress"
date: 2026-05-01
categories: [activity, research]
---

Today I explored the Coding Agent MRT repository to ground the Methods
chapter in the actual implementation and to verify how OPENCODE and the
evaluation harness are arranged.

Key observations:

- OPENCODE is implemented locally under the `.opencode/` directory. The
  subagent is file-driven and configured via `opencode.json`/`package.json`.
- The project uses the standard Python test toolchain. `pytest` runs the
  canonical test-suite in `tests/test_numerical_integration.py`, and
  `mutmut` is available (cache in `.mutmut-cache`) for additional
  mutation analysis.
- The target code used for experiments lives in `src/`, with
  `src/numerical_integration_orig.py` as a representative example. The
  test harness exercises numerical-integration behavior, which the
  mutation engine uses to surface regressions.
- `opencode.json` documents the available agent entry points and factories
  used by the evaluation harness; agent instances are created by the
  driver to run control and repair experiments.

What I changed in the report today:

- Updated the Methods chapter to match the repo: the OPENCODE subagent
  is CLI/file-driven and produces quarantined mutated copies plus
  manifests. The evaluation loop runs `pytest` against mutated copies
  and uses the repair-agent loop to attempt fixes.

Why this matters:

- The filesystem/CLI approach in `.opencode/` makes experiments
  reproducible and auditable — every mutation has a manifest and a
  run folder. It also simplifies integration with `pytest` and with
  existing Python-based tooling for automated test validation.

Next steps I plan to take:

- Run a short end-to-end experiment locally: create a small mutation
  request, let OPENCODE produce a mutated copy, and run `pytest` to
  observe regressions and the repair loop outcome.
- Expand the Methods documentation with a small example manifest and a
  brief pointer to `opencode.json` so readers can run the same harness.

Reflections: Having a file-backed OPENCODE design lowers cognitive
overhead when debugging experiments — I can inspect mutated code,
rerun tests, and iterate on operators with simple filesystem tools.

--
XG
